chemicals.concentration_notes: The overall toxicity level of these compounds in the Wandering Jew is considered mild1. The toxic compounds are concentrated specifically within the plant's sap, which is found in the leaves and stems1. The severity of the dermal reaction is dependent on the duration of exposure; prolonged contact with the sap increases the likelihood of skin redness or itchiness
chemicals.description: in cats: When a cat bites, chews, or brushes against the plant, the sap is released and directly irritates the contacted tissues1. If ingested, the sap causes mild gastrointestinal distress, leading to symptoms like drooling, vomiting, and diarrhea1. If the sap gets on the cat's fur and makes prolonged contact with the skin, it acts as a dermal irritant, causing redness and itching
chemicals.name: Irritant compounds, specifically identified as oxalates and other unspecified irritants found within the plant's sap

description: Wandering Jew (Tradescantia zebrina), a trailing houseplant with striking purple and silver striped leaves, is mildly toxic to cats. Like its relative Oyster Plant (Tradescantia spathacea), it contains irritant compounds in its sap that cause contact dermatitis on skin and GI upset when ingested.
name: Wandering Jew
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: irritant sap compounds throughout the plant; contact with broken stems releases the most concentrated sap
Mechanism: sap causes contact dermatitis; ingestion causes direct GI mucosal irritation
Symptoms: skin redness or rash from contact; vomiting, diarrhea, drooling from ingestion
The trailing purple-silver leaves are visually appealing and accessible when plants hang — relocate away from cats
Symptoms are mild and usually self-limiting; rinse affected skin with water and consult your vet if GI symptoms persist

symptoms.name: Mild vomiting, drooling, and/or diarrhea
symptoms.notes: This is generally a minor gastrointestinal upset that usually resolves on its own1. You can help by cleaning the cat's mouth with water, but you should consult a veterinarian if the vomiting becomes frequent1. 2. Skin Redness and Itching

symptoms.name: Skin redness or itch
symptoms.notes: If the sap gets on your cat, you should clean it off their fur and skin with water1. While usually a minor irritation, you should consult a veterinarian if the skin reaction appears severe
symptoms.onset: Occurs after prolonged physical contact with the plant's sap

treatments.description: Thoroughly clean the cat’s mouth with water and wash off any of the plant's sap that has gotten onto the cat's fur or skin
treatments.name: Oral and Dermal Rinsing
treatments.notes: This is the most immediate priority. It physically removes the irritant compounds (oxalates) found in the sap, which are responsible for causing drooling, mouth pain, and skin redness1. 2. Observation and Monitoring

treatments.description: Keep a close eye on the cat to monitor the progression of any mild gastrointestinal upset (such as vomiting or diarrhea) or localized skin itchiness
treatments.name: At-Home Monitoring
treatments.notes: Because the toxicity of the Wandering Jew is mild, these symptoms are usually self-limiting and will typically resolve on their own without the need for medications1. 3. Veterinary Consultation for Symptomatic Care

treatments.description: The cat is taken to a veterinarian for professional assessment, where the vet can provide specific treatments to ease persistent gastrointestinal distress or skin inflammation.
treatments.name: Veterinary Consultation and Symptomatic Treatment
treatments.notes: Professional veterinary care is generally only required if the cat's vomiting becomes frequent or if the skin reaction appears severe
